# Biology of Relapse and Drug Resistance

## Clonal Evolution and Genomic Instability

Multiple myeloma is characterized by remarkable intraclonal heterogeneity, and relapse following initial therapy reflects the Darwinian selection of resistant subclones under therapeutic pressure [@van2021Multiple]. At diagnosis, myeloma already harbors a complex subclonal architecture with competing populations carrying distinct driver mutations. Exposure to successive lines of therapy creates selective bottlenecks that eliminate drug-sensitive clones while permitting the expansion of pre-existing or newly emergent resistant populations [@Rajkumar2022Multiple]. Genomic instability, a hallmark of myeloma biology, fuels this process through ongoing chromosomal gains, losses, and structural rearrangements. Hyperdiploidy, immunoglobulin heavy chain translocations, and chromothripsis events generate the substrate for continuous clonal diversification.

Targeted sequencing of refractory myeloma has revealed a substantially altered mutational landscape compared with newly diagnosed disease. Kortum and colleagues demonstrated that relapsed/refractory myeloma harbors an increased prevalence of RAS pathway mutations (KRAS, NRAS, and/or BRAF) in 72% of patients, alongside TP53 mutations in 26%, compared with lower frequencies at diagnosis [@Kortm2016Targeted]. Critically, many of these mutations were undetectable at earlier, drug-sensitive time points, confirming that clonal selection rather than de novo mutagenesis drives the emergence of refractory disease. The acquisition of del(17p) and biallelic TP53 inactivation at relapse confers particularly aggressive biology, with shortened progression-free and overall survival across therapeutic classes [@Rajkumar2020Multiple].

## Bone Marrow Microenvironment

The bone marrow microenvironment plays a central role in sustaining resistant clones and shielding myeloma cells from therapeutic elimination. Stromal cells, osteoclasts, and immune populations collectively provide survival signals through direct cell-cell contact and soluble factor secretion. IL-6, produced abundantly by bone marrow stromal cells, activates the JAK/STAT3 pathway and serves as a master survival cytokine for myeloma cells [@van2021Multiple]. Adhesion of myeloma cells to fibronectin and stromal components activates cell adhesion-mediated drug resistance, reducing sensitivity to conventional chemotherapy and proteasome inhibitors alike.

Immune evasion within the microenvironment further contributes to relapse. Progressive T-cell exhaustion, expansion of regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and upregulation of immune checkpoint ligands (PD-L1) create an immunosuppressive milieu that limits endogenous antitumor surveillance [@Krejcik2016Daratumumab]. This immune dysfunction also affects the efficacy of T-cell-redirecting therapies at relapse, as exhausted effector populations may mount suboptimal responses even when appropriately redirected to tumor targets.

## Mechanisms of Drug-Class-Specific Resistance

### Immunomodulatory Drug Resistance

Resistance to immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs) such as lenalidomide and pomalidomide centers on the cereblon (CRBN) pathway. IMiDs bind CRBN, the substrate receptor of the CRL4-CRBN E3 ubiquitin ligase complex, redirecting it to degrade the transcription factors Ikaros (IKZF1) and Aeros (IKZF3), which are essential for myeloma survival. Mutations or downregulation of CRBN abrogate this mechanism entirely. Targeted sequencing has identified CRBN mutations in 12% and CRBN pathway gene mutations in an additional 10% of relapsed/refractory patients, with these alterations enriched specifically in IMiD-refractory disease [@Kortm2016Targeted]. Functional studies have confirmed that introduction of these mutations into myeloma cell lines confers lenalidomide resistance in vitro, establishing a direct causal link between CRBN pathway disruption and clinical IMiD failure.

### Proteasome Inhibitor Resistance

Resistance to proteasome inhibitors (PIs) such as bortezomib, carfilzomib, and ixazomib involves multiple mechanisms including mutations in the PSMB5 gene encoding the beta-5 catalytic subunit of the proteasome, upregulation of proteasome assembly and activity, and activation of alternative protein disposal pathways including autophagy and the aggresome [@Manasanch2017Proteasome]. The unfolded protein response and heat shock protein networks provide additional buffering capacity, enabling myeloma cells to manage proteotoxic stress despite partial proteasome inhibition. Notably, carfilzomib retains activity in a subset of bortezomib-refractory patients due to its irreversible binding mechanism, as demonstrated in the ENDEAVOR trial where carfilzomib-dexamethasone achieved superior PFS compared with bortezomib-dexamethasone (18.7 versus 9.4 months; HR 0.53) [@Dimopoulos2016Carfilzomib].

### Anti-CD38 Antibody Resistance

Resistance to anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies (daratumumab, isatuximab) represents an increasingly relevant clinical challenge as these agents move into frontline therapy. The primary mechanism involves downregulation of CD38 surface expression on myeloma cells, reducing antibody binding and effector functions including complement-dependent cytotoxicity and antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity [@Van2018CD]. Additionally, selection of CD38-low subclones, upregulation of complement inhibitory proteins (CD55, CD59), and depletion of NK cells during prolonged anti-CD38 exposure all contribute to treatment failure. Gandhi and colleagues demonstrated that patients with myeloma refractory to anti-CD38 antibodies have particularly poor outcomes, with a median overall survival of only 8.6 months and an overall response rate of 31% to subsequent therapies [@Gandhi2019Outcomes]. This underscores the urgent need for therapies directed at alternative targets, including BCMA, GPRC5D, and FcRH5, for patients who have exhausted CD38-directed approaches.

## Implications for Treatment Sequencing

Understanding these resistance mechanisms has direct implications for rational therapy sequencing. The non-overlapping resistance pathways of different drug classes support the combination of agents with distinct mechanisms of action, while the identification of specific resistance mutations may guide biomarker-driven treatment selection in the relapsed setting [@Dimopoulos2021Multiple]. As the therapeutic landscape expands with immunotherapies targeting novel antigens, antigen loss and immune evasion are emerging as additional resistance paradigms that will shape future treatment strategies [@Samur2021Biallelic].
